summary: McKenzie provides a transparent accounting of his first software business's early finances. With a single sale of $24.95 and expenses totaling $66.02, he's deeply in the red. He evaluates each marketing channel — abandoning eBay auctions despite views, questioning Yahoo search ads, and noting Google drives half his trial downloads. The post captures the unglamorous reality of bootstrapping a micro-ISV with roughly 50 hours invested.
key_points: First sale netted $23.93 after PayPal fees against $66.02 in expenses, showing the harsh early economics of a solo software business
eBay proved ineffective despite generating views — people weren't willing to pay his price, leading him to cut losses
Google was the standout marketing channel, driving roughly 50% of trial downloads at $30/month, while Yahoo underperformed
About 50 hours of total work invested, putting the effective hourly rate deep into negative territory
money_quotes: People on eBay are just not willing to pay any price I'm willing to charge (I get 20+ views per auction but no sales).
I just can't stand them next to Google and their performance compared with Google sucks.
$30 — my expected first month expense of working with Google, which drives probably 50% of my trial downloads.
key_insight: The earliest days of a bootstrapped software business are defined by methodical experimentation with marketing channels and honest accounting of losses, not overnight success.
